title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Helen Price, who organises volunteers at a city museum.
audio_script: Interviewer: Today we're talking to Helen Price, who organises volunteers at a city museum. Helen, how did you first become involved with the museum? || Guest: I used to bring my children here on rainy Saturdays. After they grew up, I missed the place. [20]I saw a notice asking for weekend volunteers, and I applied because I wanted to meet people and use my free time well[/20]. I thought I'd only help for a few months, but that was eight years ago. || Interviewer: What makes a good museum volunteer? || Guest: People think you need to know everything about history, but that isn't true. Training helps with facts. [21]The most important quality is being friendly enough to welcome visitors who may feel unsure or lost[/21]. Some visitors are nervous about asking questions. A smile at the entrance can make a big difference to their day. || Interviewer: What kind of task do volunteers usually enjoy most? || Guest: Many like helping with school groups, but [22]most say their favourite task is handling old objects and showing them to visitors safely[/22]. It makes history feel real. Of course, they wear gloves and follow rules, but visitors are always excited when they can look closely at something that is not behind glass. || Interviewer: Has the museum changed since you started? || Guest: Very much. We used to have printed labels and a few talks. Now there are digital screens and audio guides. [23]The change I like best is that visitors can now hear local people telling stories connected with the objects[/23]. It gives the museum a warmer feeling, especially for children who may not enjoy reading long labels. || Interviewer: What is difficult about organising volunteers? || Guest: Everyone is generous with their time, but they also have busy lives. [24]The challenge is making sure enough volunteers are available during holidays, when the museum is busiest[/24]. Students go home, grandparents look after children, and people travel. I have to plan months ahead and still make last-minute phone calls. || Interviewer: What are your plans for this year? || Guest: We are starting evening openings once a month. [25]I hope this will attract people who work during the day and never usually get a chance to visit[/25]. We'll need extra volunteers, but the building looks beautiful at night, and it will be lovely to share it with a different audience. || Interviewer: What do you personally get from volunteering? || Guest: It keeps me learning. Every month I discover something about the city that I didn't know before, and I meet people I would never normally meet. Retired engineers, students, parents and artists all volunteer with us, so the conversations are wonderful. I also like seeing visitors who arrive for a quick look and stay much longer than planned. Museums can feel quiet from the outside, but inside they are full of stories, and helping people find those stories is very satisfying. We are also trying to welcome more young volunteers. They bring energy and new ideas, especially about social media, while older volunteers often know wonderful local stories to share with them.  That mix of ages makes the volunteer team stronger and more welcoming.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Today we're talking to Helen Price, who organises volunteers at a city museum. Helen, how did you first become involved with the museum?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I used to bring my children here on rainy Saturdays. After they grew up, I missed the place. I saw a notice asking for weekend volunteers, and I applied because I wanted to meet people and use my free time well. I thought I'd only help for a few months, but that was eight years ago.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What makes a good museum volunteer?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People think you need to know everything about history, but that isn't true. Training helps with facts. The most important quality is being friendly enough to welcome visitors who may feel unsure or lost. Some visitors are nervous about asking questions. A smile at the entrance can make a big difference to their day.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What kind of task do volunteers usually enjoy most?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Many like helping with school groups, but most say their favourite task is handling old objects and showing them to visitors safely. It makes history feel real. Of course, they wear gloves and follow rules, but visitors are always excited when they can look closely at something that is not behind glass.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has the museum changed since you started?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Very much. We used to have printed labels and a few talks. Now there are digital screens and audio guides. The change I like best is that visitors can now hear local people telling stories connected with the objects. It gives the museum a warmer feeling, especially for children who may not enjoy reading long labels.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What is difficult about organising volunteers?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Everyone is generous with their time, but they also have busy lives. The challenge is making sure enough volunteers are available during holidays, when the museum is busiest. Students go home, grandparents look after children, and people travel. I have to plan months ahead and still make last-minute phone calls.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans for this year?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: We are starting evening openings once a month. I hope this will attract people who work during the day and never usually get a chance to visit. We'll need extra volunteers, but the building looks beautiful at night, and it will be lovely to share it with a different audience.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you personally get from volunteering?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It keeps me learning. Every month I discover something about the city that I didn't know before, and I meet people I would never normally meet. Retired engineers, students, parents and artists all volunteer with us, so the conversations are wonderful. I also like seeing visitors who arrive for a quick look and stay much longer than planned. Museums can feel quiet from the outside, but inside they are full of stories, and helping people find those stories is very satisfying. We are also trying to welcome more young volunteers. They bring energy and new ideas, especially about social media, while older volunteers often know wonderful local stories to share with them.  That mix of ages makes the volunteer team stronger and more welcoming.

questions.question: Why did Helen first volunteer at the museum?

options.A: She wanted to meet people in her free time.
options.B: Her children asked her to help there.
options.C: She needed experience for a history course.

questions.explanation: Helen says she applied because she wanted to meet people and use her free time well.

questions.question: What does Helen think volunteers need most?
questions.explanation: She says the most important quality is being friendly enough to welcome visitors.

questions.question: What activity do volunteers often prefer?
questions.explanation: Helen says most volunteers enjoy handling old objects and showing them to visitors safely.

questions.question: Which museum change does Helen like best?

options.A: Visitors can hear local people's stories.
options.C: Children can use more computer games.

questions.explanation: She likes that visitors can hear local stories connected with objects.

questions.question: What is Helen's main organising problem?

options.B: finding enough volunteers at busy times

questions.explanation: Helen says the challenge is making sure enough volunteers are available during holidays.

questions.question: Why is the museum starting evening openings?

options.B: to train new volunteers after work
options.C: to attract people who are busy in the daytime

questions.explanation: Helen hopes evening openings will attract people who work during the day.
